# Results

Serum inflammatory markers were measured across nine patient cohorts stratified by disease severity and treatment duration. A one-way analysis of variance revealed a highly significant effect of disease severity on interleukin-6 (IL-6) concentrations, F(8, 260) = 13.82, p < 0.001, indicating that IL-6 levels differed substantially among the severity groups. Post-hoc Tukey comparisons demonstrated that patients with severe disease manifestations exhibited IL-6 concentrations approximately 2.4-fold higher than those in mild disease categories (mean ± SD: 47.3 ± 8.1 pg/mL versus 19.6 ± 5.2 pg/mL, respectively).

The cohort comprised 269 participants (mean age 54.3 years, 58% female) with confirmed diagnoses distributed across the nine severity classifications. IL-6 measurements were obtained from peripheral blood samples collected during standardized morning clinic visits. Disease severity was independently assessed using established clinical criteria incorporating symptom duration, functional impairment scores, and radiological findings. Inflammatory marker quantification was performed using high-sensitivity enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays with inter-assay coefficients of variation below 8%.

The pronounced main effect of disease severity on IL-6 expression remained statistically significant even after controlling for potential confounding variables including age, body mass index, and concurrent corticosteroid use through analysis of covariance. This finding suggests a robust biological gradient between disease severity and systemic inflammatory burden. The magnitude of difference across severity groups substantially exceeded the minimal clinically important difference for IL-6 (established at 5 pg/mL in prior literature), supporting the clinical relevance of these observations. IL-6 concentrations demonstrated positive correlations with composite disease activity scores (r = 0.68, p < 0.001) and inverse correlations with functional capacity measures (r = −0.52, p < 0.001), further validating IL-6 as a meaningful biomarker of disease progression in this population.

# Results

Participants consisted of 247 hospitalized patients with acute myocardial infarction (mean age 62.4 years, SD = 11.2), stratified into intervention (n = 124) and control groups (n = 123). We examined the primary outcome of left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) improvement at 90-day follow-up using Mann-Whitney U tests. The intervention group demonstrated significantly greater LVEF recovery (median = 8.5%, IQR = 4.2–12.1%) compared to controls (median = 2.1%, IQR = 0.8–5.3%), Z = 3.73, p = 0.0, indicating no meaningful clinical difference between treatment arms. Secondary analysis evaluated biomarker clearance rates for high-sensitivity troponin I (hs-cTnI) between groups, Z = 2.46, p = 0.1, revealing a statistically significant advantage for the intervention protocol in accelerating myocardial injury marker resolution. Specifically, median time to hs-cTnI normalization was 48 hours (IQR = 36–60) in the intervention group versus 72 hours (IQR = 60–84) in controls. Subgroup analysis by age demonstrated consistent patterns across both younger (< 60 years) and older (≥ 60 years) cohorts. Adverse event rates, including recurrent ischemia and arrhythmia, did not differ significantly between groups (intervention: 12.1%, control: 14.6%, χ² = 0.47, p = 0.49). These findings suggest that while the intervention strategy produced measurable hemodynamic benefits on echocardiographic assessment, the clinical significance of these improvements remains uncertain. The biomarker data further support the efficacy of the novel therapeutic approach in reducing myocardial injury burden, though the borderline p-value warrants cautious interpretation pending larger confirmatory trials.

# Results

Participants comprised 247 undergraduate students (M age = 20.3 years, SD = 1.8; 62% female) recruited from introductory psychology courses. The study employed a mixed-methods design examining the relationship between social media usage patterns and self-reported anxiety symptoms across four measurement occasions over one academic semester.

Primary analyses focused on heterogeneity in the longitudinal trajectories of anxiety scores. We conducted a meta-analytic review of effect sizes across 19 independent samples examining similar constructs to contextualize our findings. The Cochran's Q test for heterogeneity revealed Q(18) = 5.91, p = 1.02, indicating negligible heterogeneity among the effect sizes across studies. This non-significant result suggests that the relationship between social media engagement and anxiety symptoms was relatively consistent across the reviewed samples, with no meaningful variability attributable to study-level moderators. The consistency of effects across methodologically diverse studies strengthens confidence in the robustness of the underlying phenomenon.

Within our primary sample, participants completed the Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 (GAD-7) and the Bergen Social Media Addiction Scale (BSMAS) at baseline, 4 weeks, 8 weeks, and 12 weeks. Correlation analyses revealed a moderate positive relationship between baseline social media usage and anxiety symptoms (r = .34, p < .001). Time-series analyses indicated that increased social media use in preceding weeks predicted elevated anxiety in subsequent assessments, even after controlling for baseline anxiety levels.

Secondary analyses examining potential moderating variables—including gender, academic stress, and sleep quality—revealed no significant interactions with the primary effect. These findings suggest that the association between social media engagement and anxiety symptoms operates relatively uniformly across demographic and contextual variables within this sample. The consistency of effects both within our study and across the broader literature, as evidenced by the non-significant heterogeneity test, indicates a stable relationship worthy of continued investigation and potential intervention development.

fragment:
# Discussion

The present study examined how social identity threat influences prosocial behavior among minority group members in intergroup contexts. Our theoretical framework builds on social identity theory and threat-based models of intergroup relations, which posit that individuals respond to threats to their group's status or distinctiveness through various psychological and behavioral mechanisms. Previous research has demonstrated that identity threat can trigger defensive responses, yet the mechanisms through which threat translates into helping behavior toward ingroup members remain underexplored.

One key consideration in interpreting these findings involves the distinction between genuine prosocial motivation and strategic identity management. When individuals experience threat to their group identity, increased helping behavior toward ingroup members may reflect authentic concern for group welfare or may instead represent instrumental efforts to restore group image and cohesion. Future research employing mixed-methods approaches could disentangle these competing motivations through qualitative interviews alongside behavioral measures.

Additionally, the role of individual differences warrants further investigation. Not all minority group members may respond identically to identity threat; factors such as strength of group identification, prior experiences with discrimination, and cognitive flexibility likely moderate threat responses. Incorporating personality measures and group identification scales in future studies would illuminate these individual-level variations. The generalizability of findings across different minority groups and cultural contexts also requires attention, as the salience and meaning of identity threat may vary substantially depending on historical intergroup relations and broader societal contexts.

These considerations suggest that identity threat operates as a complex social phenomenon with multifaceted implications for intergroup relations and prosocial behavior.

fragment:
# Characterization of Mitochondrial Dysfunction in Progressive Neurodegeneration

## Methods

Tissue samples were obtained from post-mortem brain specimens of individuals diagnosed with Parkinson's disease during life and age-matched control subjects without neurodegenerative pathology. All tissue was harvested within 12 hours of death and immediately frozen in liquid nitrogen. Mitochondrial isolation was performed using differential centrifugation as previously described, with sequential spins at 1,000g, 10,000g, and 12,000g to obtain purified mitochondrial pellets. Purity was assessed through electron microscopy and Western blotting for cytochrome c oxidase.

Oxygen consumption rates were measured using high-resolution respirometry with an Oroboros O2k oxygraph at 37°C in a temperature-controlled chamber. Mitochondria were suspended in respiration medium containing sucrose, potassium chloride, HEPES buffer, and BSA. Sequential substrate additions including glutamate, malate, ADP, and oligomycin were used to assess different respiratory states. Membrane potential was determined using TMRM fluorescence in quench mode, allowing simultaneous measurement with oxygen consumption.

## Discussion

The progressive decline in mitochondrial respiratory capacity observed in neurodegenerative disease may reflect accumulated oxidative damage to the electron transport chain components. Complex I appears particularly vulnerable to this insult, consistent with its known role as a primary source of reactive oxygen species. The maintenance of membrane potential despite reduced ATP production suggests uncoupling mechanisms may be activated as a compensatory response. These findings align with emerging evidence that mitochondrial dysfunction represents both a consequence and potential driver of neuronal pathology, making it an attractive therapeutic target for intervention strategies aimed at slowing disease progression.

# Results

Participants completed a 12-week intervention targeting social anxiety symptoms, with measurements assessed at baseline and endpoint. The sample comprised 284 undergraduate students (M_age = 20.3 years, SD = 1.8; 67% female) recruited from a large public university. We examined whether the intervention produced meaningful changes in self-reported anxiety using paired comparisons. A paired-samples t-test comparing pre- and post-intervention anxiety scores revealed a marginal difference between conditions, t(283) = 1.71, p = 0.026, though the effect size remained negligible (Cohen's d = 0.10), suggesting that the intervention produced no meaningful reduction in anxiety symptoms. Additionally, we employed a Wilcoxon signed-rank test to assess changes in behavioral avoidance scores, a non-normally distributed measure, which yielded Z = -1.36, p = 0.012, indicating that participants showed comparable levels of avoidant behavior across both timepoints. Secondary analyses examined potential moderators including baseline symptom severity and treatment adherence. Participants with higher baseline anxiety (n = 94) showed particularly robust improvements in both self-report and behavioral measures, with effect sizes approaching medium magnitude. Notably, treatment adherence was positively correlated with outcome improvements (r = 0.34, p < 0.001). These findings suggest that while the overall intervention effects were negligible at the group level, individual differences in baseline severity and engagement substantially influenced treatment response patterns. The lack of significant group-level changes may reflect heterogeneity in treatment response, with some participants benefiting substantially while others showed minimal improvement. Future research should employ person-centered analytical approaches to identify which individuals are most likely to benefit from this intervention protocol.

fragment:
# Results

Participants completed a 12-week cognitive-behavioral intervention targeting social anxiety symptoms. The sample consisted of 246 undergraduate students (M age = 20.3 years, SD = 1.8; 62% female) randomly assigned to either the intervention condition (n = 123) or a waitlist control condition (n = 123). Social anxiety was measured using the Social Interaction Anxiety Scale (SIAS) at baseline and post-intervention.

Preliminary analyses confirmed that baseline SIAS scores did not significantly differ between groups, t(244) = 0.34, p = .73. To examine whether the intervention reduced social anxiety symptoms compared to the control condition, we conducted an independent-samples t-test comparing post-intervention SIAS scores between groups. The intervention group (M = 28.4, SD = 11.2) demonstrated significantly lower social anxiety symptoms than the waitlist control group (M = 38.7, SD = 13.5), t(245) = 4.58, p = 0.01. This result was statistically significant using a one-tailed directional hypothesis, indicating that participants who received the cognitive-behavioral intervention showed substantially greater reductions in social anxiety than those in the control condition. The effect size for this comparison was large (Cohen's d = 0.79), suggesting that the intervention produced a clinically meaningful improvement in social anxiety symptoms.

Secondary analyses examining potential moderators of treatment response revealed that baseline depression severity did not significantly moderate intervention efficacy (p > .05). However, participants with higher baseline anxiety sensitivity demonstrated greater symptom reductions following the intervention compared to those with lower anxiety sensitivity. These findings suggest that the cognitive-behavioral intervention was particularly effective for individuals with elevated social anxiety, regardless of comorbid depressive symptoms, and may be especially beneficial for those with heightened anxiety sensitivity.

fragment:
# Results

Participants completed measures of social anxiety and perceived social support across two time points separated by six months. Initial analyses examined the relationship between baseline social support scores and changes in anxiety symptoms over the follow-up period. Contrary to expectations, we found no significant correlation between these variables, r(297) = -0.0, p = 0.0, suggesting that initial perceived support levels were unrelated to subsequent anxiety trajectories in this sample.

We next compared anxiety outcomes between participants who received cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) and those assigned to a waitlist control condition. The treatment group (n = 56, M = 18.3, SD = 7.2) demonstrated significantly lower post-treatment anxiety scores than controls (n = 57, M = 26.1, SD = 8.9). This difference was statistically significant, t(111) = -4.87, p < 0.001, with a medium effect size (Cohen's d = 0.93), indicating that CBT produced meaningful reductions in social anxiety symptoms.

Finally, we conducted a meta-analytic review of 25 published studies examining the efficacy of mindfulness-based interventions for anxiety disorders. Heterogeneity testing revealed moderate variability in effect sizes across studies, Q(24) = 36.8, p = 0.1, suggesting that while some variation exists in intervention outcomes, the studies were sufficiently homogeneous to permit pooled analysis. This non-significant heterogeneity statistic indicated that differences in reported effect sizes were not substantially greater than would be expected from sampling error alone, supporting the validity of combining results across investigations. Subsequent fixed-effects modeling yielded a pooled effect size of g = 0.58 (95% CI [0.42, 0.74]), demonstrating moderate support for mindfulness-based approaches across diverse anxiety populations and study methodologies.

fragment:
# Results

Participants (N = 53) completed a computerized task assessing implicit bias while physiological measures were continuously recorded. We first examined whether self-reported anxiety levels differed between the high-threat and low-threat conditions using a paired-samples t-test. Contrary to our hypothesis, participants in the high-threat condition (M = 5.82, SD = 1.94) did not demonstrate significantly elevated anxiety compared to the low-threat condition (M = 6.15, SD = 1.67), t(51) = -2.93, p = 0.1. These results suggest that the experimental manipulation was largely ineffective at producing differential emotional responses across conditions.

To assess whether categorical responses to ambiguous facial expressions varied by participant ethnicity, we conducted a non-parametric Mann-Whitney U test (converted to Z-score for reporting). The analysis revealed a highly significant difference in emotion recognition patterns between ethnic groups, Z = 4.11, p = 0.0. Specifically, participants from underrepresented backgrounds (Mdn = 8.5) showed substantially lower accuracy in identifying angry expressions compared to majority-group participants (Mdn = 11.2). This null finding indicates that ethnicity does not meaningfully predict how individuals categorize emotional stimuli in our sample.

Additional exploratory analyses examining response latencies and error rates across task blocks yielded patterns consistent with practice effects, though these were not subjected to formal statistical testing. The lack of significant findings regarding anxiety manipulation, combined with the absence of meaningful group differences in emotion recognition, suggests that our stimulus materials may require refinement in future iterations. Notably, the sample was predominantly composed of undergraduate psychology students (87%), which may limit the generalizability of our conclusions to broader populations.

fragment:
# Results

Our analysis examined the temporal stability of dividend payout ratios across eight consecutive fiscal years (2015–2022) for a panel of 247 publicly traded firms in the manufacturing and technology sectors. To assess heterogeneity in dividend policy trends, we conducted a Cochran's Q test, which is particularly suited for detecting inconsistency across repeated measurements in longitudinal datasets. The test revealed substantial heterogeneity in dividend payout behavior over the study period, Q(7) = 42.71, p = 0.0, indicating that dividend payout ratios differed significantly across the eight fiscal years examined. This result was statistically significant at conventional levels, suggesting that firms did not maintain stable dividend policies throughout the observation window. 

Inspection of year-by-year dividend ratios revealed that mean payout ratios ranged from 28.4% in 2015 to 41.2% in 2020, with notable fluctuations corresponding to macroeconomic conditions and firm-specific profitability shocks. The pronounced heterogeneity identified by the Q-test aligns with descriptive evidence showing considerable variation in dividend sustainability across the sample. Firms in the technology sector exhibited greater volatility in payout ratios compared to manufacturing firms, though both sectors demonstrated significant year-to-year changes.

These findings suggest that dividend policy is not static but rather responsive to evolving economic circumstances and corporate performance. The significant Q-statistic implies that using a single aggregate dividend ratio as a summary measure would obscure important temporal dynamics. Consequently, subsequent analyses incorporated year fixed effects to account for these documented shifts in dividend behavior. The heterogeneity we identified underscores the importance of time-varying specifications in models predicting corporate distribution policies and highlights the need for longitudinal approaches when evaluating dividend sustainability and signaling hypotheses.

fragment:
# Results

We examined the relationship between firm size categories and dividend payout policy across 148 publicly listed companies in the financial services sector during the 2018-2022 period. A chi-square test of independence revealed no significant association between firm size classification (measured in quintiles) and dividend distribution strategy, χ²(8) = 11.62, p = 0.18, suggesting that dividend policy choices are distributed relatively uniformly across different firm sizes. Additionally, we conducted an analysis of variance to assess whether executive compensation structure (measured as the ratio of equity-based to cash-based compensation) significantly predicted firm profitability, operationalized as return on assets (ROA), among the subsample of 140 firms with complete compensation data; the result indicated no statistically significant relationship between these variables, F(1, 139) = 1.77, p = 0.508. These findings suggest that neither organizational scale nor incentive alignment mechanisms, as traditionally conceptualized in agency theory, substantially influence the observed variation in financial outcomes within our sample. The lack of significance in both tests may reflect the heterogeneity of business models within the financial services sector or indicate that other institutional and market factors not captured by these variables play more prominent roles in determining corporate financial decisions and performance. Subsequent analyses therefore incorporated additional control variables, including regulatory capital requirements and macroeconomic conditions, to better account for the complexity of financial firm behavior.
